The Agent Flow platform is designed to handle long-form documents without sending entire files into the language model context window. Instead, uploaded PDF, TXT, and Markdown files are parsed, split into overlapping chunks, and stored with vector embeddings in PostgreSQL using the pgvector extension. This approach keeps prompts within token limits while still allowing the agent to retrieve the most relevant passages at query time.

Chunking uses a recursive character text splitter with a default chunk size of eight hundred characters and one hundred characters of overlap between adjacent chunks. Overlap preserves continuity across chunk boundaries so that sentences or concepts split at an edge still appear in at least one complete chunk. The splitter prefers breaking on paragraph boundaries, then newlines, then spaces, before falling back to arbitrary character splits when necessary.

During ingestion, each document receives a unique identifier in the documents table. Every chunk is inserted into document_chunks with its index, raw text content, and embedding vector. Embeddings can be generated by OpenAI or by a deterministic mock provider for local development and automated tests. Similarity search ranks chunks by cosine distance and returns the top matches for injection into the agent graph as structured context with citation identifiers.

Retrieval-augmented generation connects upload, storage, and chat in a single pipeline. Users attach document identifiers to chat requests; the RAG node embeds the latest user message, searches the selected documents, and prepends retrieved excerpts inside a system message. Citations in the API response let clients show which chunks supported each answer, satisfying traceability requirements from the product specification without exposing full source files in every response.
